mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes-Benz E300d engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned Mercedes-Benz E300d engine (OM654) typically costs £3,500–£6,000 supply only, varying by generation: pre-facelift £3,500–£6,000, facelift £4,000–£7,000. Fitting adds £1,200–£1,800. A camshaft/follower replacement costs £1,500–£4,500. Check the Era Map above for your engine code.

The OM654 engine's reliability is very much dependent on how well it has been maintained. Some owners have reported issues with wear to the valvetrain including camshafts and tappets, while others have passed 150,000–200,000 miles without problems. Mercedes changed the specification of some internal parts on later models, so post-2020 cars are more robust. A post-2020 facelift car with full Mercedes history is the buy.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes-Benz E300d?

acceptedAnswer.text: Camshaft and cam-follower wear (top-end rattle on cold start, £1,500–£4,500), injector seal leaks ('black death', £600–£1,200), DPF/EGR issues (£400–£1,200), AdBlue system failure (£500–£1,500), and 9G-Tronic gearbox issues (£800–£2,500).

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes-Benz E300d engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: E-Class values range from £8,000–£35,000 depending on generation, condition and mileage. A £3,500–£6,000 reconditioned engine represents 10–80% of vehicle value — our 70% Mercedes-Benz scrap threshold means careful evaluation is required. For a well-maintained facelift (2020+) example, replacement is often justified. For an ageing pre-facelift with other issues, assess scrap value first.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes-Benz E300d engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The E300d uses the OM654 engine code — Mercedes' 2.0-litre four-cylinder diesel producing 261 bhp. The pre-facelift (2016–2020) and facelift (2020–2023) share the same engine code but with internal revisions. Check your VIN or registration year. The code is stamped on the engine block.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the camshaft wear issue, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — if caught early. Camshaft and cam-follower replacement costs £1,500–£4,500 and is significantly cheaper than a full engine replacement (£3,500–£6,000). The one fault worth knowing in detail is camshaft and cam-follower wear on the OM654 diesel, reported by some owners at higher mileages. At the first sign of top-end rattle on cold start, book an inspection immediately. More frequent oil changes will help keep the engine in good health.

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect current market ranges across both E300d generations. Reconditioned units for the facelift (2020+) typically command a premium due to revised internal parts. Reconditioned engine costs typically range from £1,200 to £2,200 supply only, with premium marques commanding higher costs.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an E300d engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £75–£110/hr (independent Mercedes specialist) or £130–£180/hr (London/SE dealer). The OM654's compact four-cylinder layout makes it relatively accessible.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Mercedes-Benz E300d — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Preventative — if caught before catastrophic failure
Forced regen or cleaning — worth it
Viable — valve body or TCU repair often possible

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Mercedes-Benz E300d? Check These First
buyIf: * Post-2020 facelift model — revised internal parts improve reliability
* Full Mercedes service history with regular oil changes (more frequent than the standard schedule is better)
* Documented camshaft inspection history on higher-mileage examples
* A post-2020 facelift E220d or E300d with full Mercedes history is the buy
avoidIf: * Any top-end rattle on cold start — camshaft/cam-follower wear risk
* AdBlue warning light without a documented repair
* Poor service history — the OM654 is unforgiving of neglect
* Evidence of DPF issues without documented diagnosis

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The E300d is the refined diesel executive saloon that sets the benchmark. The OM654 engine is generally reliable when properly maintained, but the one fault worth knowing in detail is camshaft and cam-follower wear reported by some owners at higher mileages. A post-2020 facelift car with full Mercedes history is the buy; a tired diesel with no paperwork is the one to leave on the forecourt. Buy on history, maintain the valvetrain and emissions systems, and the E300d will reward you.

cta.label: → Compare Mercedes-Benz E300d engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes-Benz E300d (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for any top-end rattle on startup (camshaft/cam-follower wear warning)
* Service History — Check the year to identify which generation — pre-2020 vs post-2020 facelift
* Camshaft History — Any top-end work done? Camshaft/follower inspection or replacement?
* Oil Change History — More frequent oil changes (every 8,000–10,000 miles) are better
* AdBlue System — Any warnings? Check AdBlue level and age; tank replacement is expensive
* DPF History — Ask about driving patterns; frequent short trips cause DPF issues
* EGR System — Any coolant loss or rough idle? EGR clogging is common
* Gearbox Behaviour — Check for smooth shifts; 9G-Tronic issues are known
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Resolve before buying
